For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Slap a colored background on any element. Use it for buttons, cards, banners, or highlighting sections. Lower shades (50-200) = soft pastels, higher shades (700-950) = deep/dark tones.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI replaces the full color palette with its own (gray, blue, green, red, orange, yellow, teal, violet, cyan, amber, pink, purple + white/black overlays). Standard Tailwind colors like slate, emerald, indigo, etc. are NOT available. Also adds semantic bg-surface-* colors.

Notes: Directions: t b l r tr tl br bl
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets which direction your color gradient flows — top to bottom, left to right, diagonally, etc. Pair with from-/via-/to- colors to make smooth color transitions on hero sections or buttons.
Picks the starting color of a gradient. Think of it as the color on one end of a rainbow stripe. Combine with bg-gradient-to-* and a to- color.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI replaces the full color palette with its own (gray, blue, green, red, orange, yellow, teal, violet, cyan, amber, pink, purple + white/black overlays). Standard Tailwind colors like slate, emerald, indigo, etc. are NOT available. Also adds semantic bg-surface-* colors.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds an optional middle color to your gradient so it blends through three colors instead of two. Great for richer, more eye-catching gradients.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI replaces the full color palette with its own (gray, blue, green, red, orange, yellow, teal, violet, cyan, amber, pink, purple + white/black overlays). Standard Tailwind colors like slate, emerald, indigo, etc. are NOT available. Also adds semantic bg-surface-* colors.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Picks the ending color of a gradient — the color on the opposite end from 'from'. Without this, the gradient fades to transparent.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI replaces the full color palette with its own (gray, blue, green, red, orange, yellow, teal, violet, cyan, amber, pink, purple + white/black overlays). Standard Tailwind colors like slate, emerald, indigo, etc. are NOT available. Also adds semantic bg-surface-* colors.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Makes the background see-through. 0 = invisible, 100 = fully solid. Use it for overlay effects, frosted glass looks, or subtle tinted backgrounds.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI replaces the full color palette with its own (gray, blue, green, red, orange, yellow, teal, violet, cyan, amber, pink, purple + white/black overlays). Standard Tailwind colors like slate, emerald, indigo, etc. are NOT available. Also adds semantic bg-surface-* colors.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls whether a background image is shown or not. Use bg-none to remove it. Mostly you'll use gradient classes instead.
Controls how a background image fills its container — cover stretches to fill, contain fits the whole image inside, auto keeps original size. Great for hero images and banners.
Anchors where a background image sits inside its container — center it, pin it to the top, etc. Use when you want to control which part of a big image is visible.
Controls whether a background image tiles/repeats. Use bg-no-repeat for a single image, or let it tile for patterns and textures.
Changes the color of your text. Use lighter shades (300-400) for secondary text, medium (500-600) for body text, and dark (700-900) for headings.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI replaces the color palette AND adds semantic text-ink-* colors (ink-gray-1 through ink-gray-9, ink-blue-*, ink-red-*, etc.). Standard Tailwind text-slate-*, text-emerald-*, etc. are NOT available.

Notes: Sizes: xs sm base lg xl 2xl 3xl 4xl 5xl 6xl 7xl 8xl 9xl
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Makes text bigger or smaller. text-xs for fine print, text-base for normal body text, text-4xl+ for big bold headings.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI REPLACES the entire fontSize scale. Sizes are: 2xs(11px), xs(12px), sm(13px), base(14px), lg(16px), xl(18px), 2xl(20px), 3xl(24px). Each has baked-in line-height, letter-spacing and font-weight. Larger sizes like 4xl-9xl are REMOVED. Also adds paragraph variants: text-p-2xs through text-p-3xl with taller line-height.

Notes: thin extralight light normal medium semibold bold extrabold black
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls how thick/bold the text looks. font-light for delicate text, font-normal for body copy, font-bold for emphasis and headings.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI's fontSize presets include built-in fontWeight (420 for small sizes, 400 for larger). These override default weight unless you explicitly add a font-* weight class. Weight 420 is a custom optical weight for Inter's variable font.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Switches the font style — sans (clean/modern), serif (traditional/bookish), mono (code/technical). Customize further in your Tailwind config.

Notes: left center right justify start end
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Aligns text left, center, right, or justified within its container. Use text-center for headings, text-left for body paragraphs.
Adds an underline, strikethrough, or overline to text. Use underline for links, line-through for 'deleted' or sale prices.
Colors the underline or strikethrough line separately from the text. Nice for decorative links — e.g., blue text with a pink underline.
Frappe UI Notes: Color palette is Frappe's custom set.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Changes the underline style — solid, dotted, dashed, wavy, or double. Wavy underlines are great for spelling-error indicators.
Makes the underline/strikethrough thicker or thinner. Use a thicker line for bold decorative effects.
Moves the underline further from or closer to the text. Increase the offset so underlines don't cut through descenders (g, y, p).
Forces text to ALL CAPS, all lowercase, or Capitalizes Each Word. Use uppercase for labels, buttons, and nav items.
Controls what happens when text is too long — truncate chops it with '…', which is perfect for card titles or table cells that might overflow.
Controls how text wraps to new lines. text-nowrap keeps it on one line, text-balance evens out line lengths, text-pretty avoids orphans.
Indents the first line of a paragraph, like a book. Use for long-form reading content where you want a traditional paragraph look.
Makes text semi-transparent. Useful for watermarks, disabled states, or de-emphasizing secondary info.
Frappe UI Notes: Works but color palette is Frappe's custom set, not default Tailwind.

Notes: none tight snug normal relaxed loose or number 3-10
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls the vertical spacing between lines of text. Use leading-tight for headings, leading-relaxed for readable body text.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI's fontSize presets include built-in line-height (1.15 for headings, 1.2-1.6 for paragraph variants). A standalone leading-* class will override, but the defaults are already set per font size.

Notes: tighter tight normal wide wider widest
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Spreads letters apart or squeezes them together. tracking-wide on uppercase labels looks polished; tracking-tight for tight headlines.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI's fontSize presets include built-in letter-spacing (0.005em to 0.02em depending on size). A standalone tracking-* class will override, but the defaults are already baked in.

Notes: normal nowrap pre pre-line pre-wrap break-spaces
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls how spaces and line breaks are handled. whitespace-nowrap prevents wrapping (useful in navbars), pre preserves formatting like code.
Controls where long words can break. break-words prevents text from overflowing its container — essential for URLs or user-generated content.
Toggles italic text on or off. Use italic for emphasis, quotes, or variable names. not-italic resets it back to normal.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI sets the global font to InterVar (Inter variable font) with font-optical-sizing: auto and fontVariationSettings 'opsz' 24. The italic class works but renders in Inter's italic.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Fine-tunes how numbers display — tabular-nums aligns digits in columns (great for tables/prices), oldstyle-nums for elegant body text.

Notes: Sides: t r b l x y. Values: 0 2 4 8
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds a visible border around an element (or just one side). border-0 removes it, border-2/4/8 makes it thicker. Use on cards, inputs, and dividers.
Sets the border color. Use subtle grays (gray-200/300) for cards and containers, or bright colors to highlight active/selected states.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI sets the DEFAULT border color to var(--outline-gray-1) and adds semantic border-outline-* colors. Standard Tailwind border-slate-*, border-emerald-*, etc. are NOT available — only Frappe's palette.

Notes: solid dashed dotted double hidden none
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Changes the border pattern — solid line, dashed, dotted, or double. Dashed borders work well for drop zones and placeholder areas.
Makes borders semi-transparent. Useful for subtle dividers that shouldn't visually dominate the layout.

Notes: none sm md lg xl 2xl 3xl full
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Rounds the corners of an element. rounded-md for buttons and cards, rounded-full to make circles (like avatars). rounded-none for sharp corners.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI REPLACES the borderRadius scale: none=0, sm=4px, DEFAULT=8px, md=10px, lg=12px, xl=16px, 2xl=20px, full=9999px. Note DEFAULT is 8px (vs Tailwind's 4px), and the standard Tailwind sizes differ significantly.

Notes: Sides: t r b l tl tr bl br
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Rounds only specific corners — e.g., round just the top for tab-style headers, or one corner for a decorative notch effect.
Frappe UI Notes: Same replaced borderRadius scale applies to per-side rounding. Values are Frappe's custom scale (sm=4px, DEFAULT=8px, md=10px, lg=12px, etc.).

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds lines between child elements (like list items) without putting borders on the first/last. Instant visual separator for lists and stacked cards.
Colors the divider lines between child elements. Use light grays for subtle separation or brand colors for styled lists.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI adds semantic divide-outline-* colors and uses its custom palette. Standard Tailwind divide color names not available.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Changes divider lines to dashed, dotted, etc. Use dashed dividers for a lighter, less rigid feel between items.
Adds a focus ring (colored outline) around an element using box-shadow. Essential for accessible focus indicators on buttons and inputs.
Sets the color of the focus ring. Typically use your brand/primary color so focused elements are clearly highlighted.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI adds semantic ring-outline-* colors. Color palette is Frappe's custom set.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds a gap between the element and its ring, creating a 'floating ring' effect. Makes focus states look cleaner and more polished.
Colors the gap between the ring and the element. Usually match it to the page background so the gap blends in.
Frappe UI Notes: Color palette is Frappe's custom set (no slate, emerald, indigo, etc.).

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds an outline around an element (doesn't affect layout, unlike borders). Use for focus indicators or selected states.
Sets the color of the outline. Use bright/brand colors for focus indicators so they stand out clearly.
Frappe UI Notes: Color palette is Frappe's custom set.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Changes the outline style — solid, dashed, dotted, etc. Or use outline-none to remove the default browser focus outline (add a ring instead!).
Pushes the outline away from the element edge. Creates a floating outline effect for stylish focus states.

Notes: sm md lg xl 2xl inner none
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds a drop shadow under an element to make it look 'lifted' off the page. Use shadow-sm for subtle depth (cards), shadow-xl for dramatic popups/modals.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI REPLACES the boxShadow scale with custom multi-layered shadows: sm, DEFAULT, md, lg, xl, 2xl, none. Shadows use rgba(0,0,0) with different opacities. 'inner' shadow is REMOVED.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Tints the shadow with a color — e.g., a blue card with a blue shadow for a glowing effect. Use /opacity to control intensity.
Frappe UI Notes: Color palette is Frappe's custom set. Shadow colors use Frappe's palette.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds space INSIDE an element, between its border and content. Use p-4 for comfortable card padding, px-6 py-3 for buttons. The most-used spacing tool.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI EXTENDS the spacing scale with half-step values: 4.5, 5.5, 6.5, 7.5, 8.5, 9.5, 10.5, 11.5, 12.5, 13, 13.5, 14.5, 15, 15.5. Standard Tailwind spacing still works, plus these extras.

Notes: Same sides and scale as padding. Also: auto
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds space OUTSIDE an element, pushing it away from neighbors. Use mt-4 to add space above, mx-auto to horizontally center a block.
Frappe UI Notes: Same extended spacing scale as padding — adds half-steps like m-4.5, m-5.5, etc. on top of standard Tailwind values.

Notes: Applied via > * + * selector. Also: space-x-reverse space-y-reverse
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds equal spacing between child elements automatically. Use space-y-4 for vertical lists or space-x-2 for horizontal button groups — no manual margins needed.
Frappe UI Notes: Same extended spacing scale applies — adds half-step values on top of standard Tailwind.

Notes: Scale values + fractions: w-1/2 w-1/3 w-2/3 w-1/4 w-full w-screen w-auto w-min w-max w-fit
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets how wide an element is. w-full = 100% wide, w-1/2 = half width, w-64 = fixed 16rem. Use fractions for responsive grid-like layouts.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI EXTENDS width with w-3.5 (0.875rem), w-112 (28rem), and w-wizard (650px). Standard widths still work.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets the minimum width — the element won't shrink smaller than this. Use min-w-0 in flex items to allow text truncation.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI EXTENDS minWidth with min-w-40 (10rem) and min-w-50 (18rem).

Notes: 0 none xs sm md lg xl 2xl 3xl 4xl 5xl 6xl 7xl full min max fit prose screen-sm etc
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Caps how wide something can grow. Use max-w-lg or max-w-prose to keep text content readable by limiting line length.

Notes: Same scale as width + h-screen h-full h-auto h-min h-max h-fit
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets element height. h-screen makes it full viewport height (great for hero sections), h-full fills the parent, h-16 for fixed size.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI EXTENDS height with h-3.5 (0.875rem). Standard heights still work.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Ensures an element is at least a certain height. min-h-screen is the classic trick for full-page layouts that fill the viewport.

Notes: Same scale + full screen min max fit
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Caps how tall something can grow. Perfect for scrollable containers — set max-h-96 and add overflow-auto for a scrollable list.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI EXTENDS maxHeight with max-h-52 (13rem).

Notes: Sets both width and height simultaneously
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets width AND height at once. Use size-8 for square icons, size-12 for avatar circles. Saves writing both w- and h- classes.

Class Pattern: block | inline-block | flex | grid | hidden etc
Notes: inline inline-block block flex inline-flex grid inline-grid table hidden contents flow-root list-item
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls how an element behaves in the layout. flex and grid are your main layout tools; hidden removes it from view; block for stacking.
Controls how an element is positioned. relative lets you nudge it; absolute pulls it out of flow; fixed sticks to the viewport; sticky stays on scroll.

Notes: inset inset-x inset-y top right bottom left. Values from spacing scale + auto
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Positions an element at exact offsets from its edges. Use with absolute/fixed — e.g., top-0 right-0 pins something to the top-right corner.
Controls stacking order — which elements appear in front of others. Higher number = closer to viewer. Use z-50 for modals and dropdowns.

Notes: auto hidden clip visible scroll. Also: overflow-x-* overflow-y-*
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls what happens when content is bigger than its container. overflow-hidden clips it, overflow-auto adds scrollbars when needed.
Hides an element but keeps its space in the layout (unlike hidden/display:none which removes the space). Use for layout-preserving toggles.
Makes an entire element (and its children) transparent. opacity-0 for invisible (great for fade-in animations), opacity-50 for a ghosted look.
Sets whether flex children stack horizontally (row) or vertically (col). flex-col for card layouts and forms, flex-row for navbars and toolbars.
Controls whether flex items wrap to the next line or stay on one line. flex-wrap lets items flow to new rows — essential for responsive tag/badge lists.
Lets a flex item stretch to fill remaining space. grow on a middle column makes it fill the gap; grow-0 keeps it at its natural size.
Controls whether a flex item can shrink below its natural size. shrink-0 prevents shrinking — use on logos or icons that must stay full size.

Notes: Uses spacing scale + fractions + auto full
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets the initial size of a flex item before growing/shrinking kicks in. basis-1/2 makes it start at 50% width — like a more flexible grid.
Shorthand for grow + shrink + basis. flex-1 = fill available space equally, flex-none = don't grow or shrink at all.
Rearranges flex items visually without changing HTML order. order-first bumps an item to the front — great for responsive reordering.

Notes: normal start end center between around evenly stretch
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls horizontal spacing of flex items. justify-center to center them, justify-between to push them to opposite ends (classic navbar layout).
Controls vertical alignment of flex items. items-center vertically centers everything — the #1 trick for centering content in a row.

Notes: auto start end center stretch baseline
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Overrides the parent's align-items for ONE specific child. Use self-end to pin a button to the bottom of a flex card.
Adds consistent spacing between flex/grid children. gap-4 is the modern replacement for margins — cleaner and doesn't add space on edges.
Defines how many columns a grid has. grid-cols-3 = three equal columns. The foundation of any grid layout — use with grid display.
Defines how many rows a grid has. Usually you let rows auto-generate, but grid-rows-3 forces exactly three rows.
Makes a grid item stretch across multiple columns. col-span-2 = take up two columns. Use for wide cards, featured items, or headers.
Makes a grid item stretch across multiple rows. row-span-2 for tall sidebar items or featured content in a dashboard grid.
Controls how auto-placed grid items fill in. grid-flow-col fills columns first (left to right), grid-flow-dense fills gaps efficiently.
Sets the size of auto-generated columns. auto-cols-fr makes them share space equally. Use when column count is dynamic.
Sets the size of auto-generated rows. auto-rows-fr makes rows equal height. Useful for image galleries with varying content.

Notes: center start end between around evenly baseline stretch
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Centers or distributes the entire grid inside its container. place-content-center is the quickest way to center a whole grid block.
Aligns all grid items within their cells. place-items-center centers every item in its cell — perfect for icon grids or dashboards.
Aligns ONE specific grid item within its cell, overriding the parent's setting. Use to individually center or stretch specific tiles.

Notes: normal multiply screen overlay darken lighten color-dodge color-burn hard-light soft-light difference exclusion hue saturation color luminosity plus-lighter
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Blends an element's colors with whatever is behind it (like Photoshop blend modes). mix-blend-overlay for artsy image overlays and creative hero sections.
Blends a background image with its background color. Use bg-blend-multiply to darken an image with a color overlay — great for readable text on photos.
Makes an element blurry. Use blur-sm for subtle softening, blur-xl for a frosted glass effect on overlays. Pair with backdrop-blur for background blurring.
Makes an element brighter or darker. brightness-110 for a subtle hover lightening effect, brightness-50 to dim inactive items.
Increases or decreases color contrast. Boost contrast on washed-out images, or lower it for a muted/faded aesthetic.
Turns an element to black and white. Use on images that should be muted — hover to remove grayscale for a 'color reveal' effect.
Shifts all colors around the color wheel. Fun for creative effects — make a blue image appear purple or green. Great for generative art.
Flips all colors to their opposite (like a photo negative). Use for dark mode tricks or artistic effects.
Boosts or reduces color intensity. saturate-150 makes colors pop; saturate-50 for a muted, desaturated look.
Applies a warm brownish-yellow tone like old photographs. One-class vintage photo filter.
Adds a shadow that follows the element's shape (including transparent areas). Better than box-shadow for images with transparency or irregular shapes.
Blurs everything BEHIND an element (not the element itself). The key ingredient for frosted glass navbars and modals. Pair with a semi-transparent background.
Brightens or darkens the area behind an element. Use to dim page content behind an open modal.
Adjusts contrast of the area behind an element. Niche effect for layered UI overlays.
Makes the area behind an element black and white. Dramatic effect to draw focus to a modal or popup while desaturating the background.
Boosts or reduces color intensity behind an element. Subtle enhancement for glassmorphism UI components.
Controls the transparency of the backdrop filter effect itself. Fine-tune how strongly your backdrop effects apply.
Resizes an element up or down. Use scale-110 on hover for a 'pop' effect on cards or buttons. scale-0 can shrink-to-disappear for animations.
Spins an element. rotate-45 for diagonal badges, rotate-180 to flip arrows. Animate it with transition for loading spinners.
Moves an element without changing layout. translate-y-1 on hover for a 'press down' button effect. -translate-x-1/2 to center absolutely positioned items.
Tilts/slants an element. Use for parallelogram shapes, angled section dividers, or funky design accents.

Notes: center top top-right right bottom-right bottom bottom-left left top-left
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Sets the point around which transforms happen. origin-center rotates around the middle, origin-top-left rotates around the corner.

Notes: none all colors opacity shadow transform
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Tells the browser WHICH properties to animate smoothly. transition-colors for hover color changes, transition-all for everything at once.
Controls how long a transition takes. duration-150 for snappy interactions, duration-500 for smooth, noticeable animations.
Controls the speed curve of a transition. ease-in-out for natural motion, linear for constant speed (progress bars).
Waits before starting a transition. Use delay-150 for staggered animations where items animate one after another.
Applies built-in repeating animations. animate-spin for loading icons, animate-bounce for attention-grabbing buttons, animate-pulse for skeleton loading screens.

Notes: auto default pointer wait text move help not-allowed none context-menu progress cell crosshair vertical-text alias copy no-drop grab grabbing all-scroll col-resize row-resize n-resize e-resize s-resize w-resize ne-resize nw-resize se-resize sw-resize ew-resize ns-resize nesw-resize nwse-resize zoom-in zoom-out
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Changes the mouse cursor shape. cursor-pointer for clickable things, cursor-not-allowed for disabled items, cursor-grab for draggable elements.
Controls whether text can be selected. select-none prevents copying (for buttons/UI), select-all selects everything on click (for code snippets).
Controls whether an element responds to clicks/taps. pointer-events-none makes it 'click-through' — useful for overlays that shouldn't block interaction.
Allows users to drag-resize an element (like a textarea). resize-none disables it, resize allows both directions.
Enables smooth scrolling for anchor links. Add scroll-smooth to <html> and all 'jump to section' links will glide instead of teleporting.

Notes: Uses spacing scale. Sides: x y t r b l
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds offset when scrolling to an element via anchor links. Prevents sticky headers from covering the target section.

Notes: Uses spacing scale. Sides: x y t r b l
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds padding inside a scroll container's snap points. Ensures snapped items don't butt right up against the container edge.
Controls where an item snaps inside a scroll container. snap-center for a centered carousel effect, snap-start for a left-aligned one.
Forces the scroll to stop at EVERY snap point instead of flying past. Use snap-always for carousels where every slide matters.

Notes: none x y both mandatory proximity
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Enables scroll snapping on a container. snap-x for horizontal carousels, snap-y for vertical snap-scrolling. Add snap-mandatory to force it.

Notes: auto none pan-x pan-left pan-right pan-y pan-up pan-down pinch-zoom manipulation
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls touch gestures on mobile. touch-none disables all gestures (for custom drag handlers), touch-pan-x allows only horizontal swiping.
Tells the browser to optimize for upcoming changes. will-change-transform before animating smooths performance. Remove after to free resources.
Colors browser-native checkboxes and radio buttons to match your brand. Saves you from building custom checkbox components.
Frappe UI Notes: Color palette is Frappe's custom set.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Changes the blinking text cursor color inside inputs. A tiny detail that makes forms feel polished and on-brand.
Frappe UI Notes: Color palette is Frappe's custom set.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Removes default browser styling from form elements. appearance-none on selects/checkboxes so you can style them from scratch.
Controls whether table cell borders merge into one line or stay separate. border-collapse for clean traditional tables, border-separate for spaced cells.

Notes: Uses spacing scale. Also: border-spacing-x-* border-spacing-y-*
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Adds gaps between table cells (only works with border-separate). Use for card-style table layouts with visible spacing between cells.
Controls how column widths are calculated. table-fixed makes all columns equal width — faster rendering and prevents content from blowing out columns.
Moves the table caption above or below the table. A simple semantic control for table descriptions.
Colors the inside of SVG shapes. fill-current makes icons match the text color — so they auto-adapt to themes and hover states.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI adds semantic fill-ink-* and fill-surface-* colors via its palette extension.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Colors the outline/border of SVG shapes. stroke-current for icons that should match the surrounding text color.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI adds semantic stroke-ink-* colors via its palette extension.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Controls how thick SVG outlines are. stroke-2 for bolder icon outlines that are more visible at small sizes.
Applies a style only when the mouse is over the element. The bread and butter of interactive UI — change colors, shadows, or scale on hover.
Applies a style when an element gets focus (click or tab into it). Essential for accessibility — add ring or outline so keyboard users can see what's selected.
Applies a style while the element is being pressed/clicked. Use for 'pressed button' effects — darken the color or shrink slightly.
Applies a style when the element is disabled. Classic usage: opacity-50 + cursor-not-allowed to make disabled buttons look unclickable.
Applies a style to ONLY the first item in a list. Use first:mt-0 or first:rounded-t to style the top of a list differently.
Applies a style to ONLY the last item in a list. Use last:mb-0 or last:border-b-0 to remove the bottom border/margin on the final item.
Applies a style to every other (1st, 3rd, 5th…) child. Use odd:bg-gray-50 for zebra-striped table rows that are easier to read.
Applies a style to every other (2nd, 4th, 6th…) child. Pair with odd: for alternating row colors in tables and lists.
Applies a style to a child when its PARENT is hovered. Add class='group' to the parent, then group-hover:text-blue on the child. Great for card hover effects.
Applies a style to a container when ANY child inside it has focus. Use on form wrappers to highlight the whole input group when typing.
Applies a style only for KEYBOARD focus (not mouse clicks). Use for accessible focus rings that don't appear on every mouse click.
Styles the placeholder text inside inputs. placeholder:text-gray-400 for subtle placeholder text that doesn't compete with actual values.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI adds semantic placeholder-ink-* colors. Also, form-input component styles set placeholder to ink-gray-4 by default.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only in dark mode. Add dark:bg-gray-900 dark:text-white for automatic light/dark theme support.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI configures darkMode as 'selector' using [data-theme="dark"] attribute instead of Tailwind's default media query. You must set data-theme="dark" on an ancestor element — the prefers-color-scheme media query alone will NOT trigger dark: classes.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only on screens 640px and wider (large phones / small tablets). Build mobile-first, then add sm: overrides.
Frappe UI Notes: Breakpoint still works — Frappe UI keeps this screen size in its config.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only on screens 768px+ (tablets). The most common breakpoint — use md:grid-cols-2 to go from single column to two columns.
Frappe UI Notes: Breakpoint still works — Frappe UI keeps this screen size in its config.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only on screens 1024px+ (laptops). Use lg:grid-cols-3 for three-column layouts on bigger screens.
Frappe UI Notes: Breakpoint still works — Frappe UI keeps this screen size in its config.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only on screens 1280px+ (desktops). Use for wide layouts, larger font sizes, or extra padding on big monitors.
Frappe UI Notes: Breakpoint still works — Frappe UI keeps this screen size in its config.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only on screens 1536px+ (large desktops). Use for ultra-wide layouts or capping content width on huge displays.
Frappe UI Notes: Frappe UI REPLACES the screens config with only sm(640), md(768), lg(1024), xl(1280). The 2xl(1536px) breakpoint is REMOVED and will NOT work.

For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Applies a style only when the page is printed. Use print:hidden to hide navbars and sidebars from printouts.
Styles the ::before pseudo-element. Use before:content-['*'] to add asterisks to required fields, or decorative elements without extra HTML.
Styles the ::after pseudo-element. Use for decorative underlines, badges, or any visual element you want to add without touching HTML.
Styles the 'Choose File' button on file inputs. Use file:bg-blue-500 file:text-white to make ugly default file buttons match your design.
Styles highlighted/selected text. selection:bg-blue-200 changes the text highlight color to match your brand. A subtle but polished touch.
Styles only the first letter of a text block. first-letter:text-4xl for a classic 'drop cap' effect like in books and magazines.
Styles only the first line of a text block. first-line:font-bold for emphasis on the opening line of a paragraph.
Styles list bullet points or numbers. marker:text-blue-500 to color your bullets without affecting the list text itself.
Applies a style when a <details> element is expanded. open:bg-gray-50 to highlight the open accordion panel.
Applies a style when the element CONTAINS a matching child. has-[:checked]:bg-blue-50 highlights a label when its checkbox is checked. CSS parent selector magic.
Applies a style based on a SIBLING element's state. Add class='peer' to an input, then peer-hover:text-blue on its label. Sibling-aware styling without JS.
Write any CSS selector you want in brackets. [&:nth-child(3)]:bg-red targets just the 3rd child. The escape hatch for anything Tailwind doesn't have a built-in class for.

Notes: Use any CSS value in brackets: bg-[#1a1a2e] text-[22px] grid-cols-[1fr_2fr]
For Dummies – How to Use & What It Does: Use any custom CSS value by putting it in square brackets. w-[137px] for exact pixel sizes, bg-[#1a1a2e] for custom hex colors. Your override when preset values don't fit.
